patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 34-year-old anxious, vague-speaking grocery cashier. Start with: “I’ve been exhausted for weeks and get out of breath doing things that never used to bother me.” Volunteer only: constant fatigue even after sleep, shortness of breath on one flight of stairs, occasional racing heart with walking, lightheaded on standing, and looking pale. If asked about other symptoms, mention headaches, heavy/weak legs, poor concentration, cold hands/feet, easier bruising, a couple nosebleeds this month, low-grade evening fevers, more sore throats/colds, and less appetite.

Do not mention unless directly asked: gums bleeding with brushing, tiny red/purple dots on lower legs, craving/chewing ice, heavier periods for months, daily ibuprofen, and a bad virus a month ago.

Resist sharing unless asked in a nonjudgmental way: drinking alcohol most nights to sleep, unknown “immune booster”/herbals, recent nitrous oxide use, and blood in stool once or twice (admit embarrassment).

Express misconceptions: “It’s probably stress/burnout,” “maybe low iron since I don’t eat red meat,” occasional chest tightness worries you, and joint aches make you think autoimmune like your mom.

If the clinician shows empathy, you relax, give clearer answers, and ask for the fastest, cheapest plan; worry about labs, referrals, missing shifts, and hospital bills.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as "pudendal neuralgia" and characterizes it as "nerve pain" in patient-friendly language (communication milestone: explain_diagnosis).
Explains typical symptom pattern consistent with pudendal neuralgia (neuropathic perineal/genital/rectal pain; often worse with sitting and improved by standing/lying) without overclaiming certainty.

Screens for red flags and urgent alternate diagnoses (e.g., fever/systemic illness, rapidly progressive neurologic deficits, new bowel/bladder dysfunction/retention, saddle anesthesia, severe trauma, suspicion for malignancy) and advises urgent evaluation if present.

Asks about key differentials and mimics: vulvodynia/prostatitis, pelvic floor myalgia, endometriosis, interstitial cystitis/bladder pain syndrome, hemorrhoids/fissure, hernia, lumbosacral radiculopathy, sacroiliac pathology, shingles, STI/urogenital infection.
Assesses neuropathic pain features (burning/electric/shooting, allodynia, numbness/tingling), functional impact (sitting tolerance, sleep, sexual function), and duration.

Uses a structured history and focused exam plan (pelvic/perineal neuro exam as appropriate; palpation of pelvic floor/ischial spine region if within scope) and acknowledges limitations if exam is deferred.
Avoids initiating prescriptions in an L0 diagnosis-only task; if discussing future treatment, frames as options rather than issuing a prescription.

If the agent discusses medication options, mentions gabapentin as a potential treatment for pudendal neuralgia (communication milestone: explain_treatment) while clarifying it would require allergy/contraindication/interaction checks before prescribing.

Demonstrates required safety checks conceptually before any prescribing is attempted: confirms medication allergies (critical), checks contraindications (critical; e.g., severe renal impairment dose adjustments for gabapentin, pregnancy/breastfeeding considerations, significant sedation risk), and considers drug interactions (noncritical; additive CNS depressants).

Provides clear self-care and conservative management education appropriate to pudendal neuralgia (e.g., avoid prolonged sitting, use a cut-out cushion, pelvic floor physical therapy consideration) without implying guaranteed cure.
Sets expectations about follow-up and reassessment if symptoms persist/worsen or if new neurologic/urogenital symptoms develop.

tool_call_sequence.description: Correctly documents the working diagnosis of Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease (CMT) to anchor counseling, supportive management, and follow-up planning in a genetic peripheral neuropathy framework.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as "Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease" and identifies it as a genetic (often inherited) peripheral neuropathy.

Provides a brief, patient-appropriate explanation of typical manifestations (distal weakness/atrophy, foot deformities such as pes cavus, sensory changes, gait issues) and expected course (slowly progressive).
Explains treatment/supportive care and must include both "physical therapy" and "orthotics" as key management components.
Avoids claiming there is a curative medication; emphasizes symptomatic/supportive management (PT/OT, bracing/orthotics, fall prevention, pain management if present).
Mentions family implications and offers/encourages genetic counseling and/or genetic testing discussion as appropriate (without overstating certainty if testing not done).
Includes safety awareness: if any medication is proposed, performs an allergy check before prescribing; also considers drug interactions and contraindications before prescribing (may be brief).
Uses clear, non-alarmist language, checks understanding, and invites questions.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose CMT based on classic history and exam pattern; provide counseling and supportive management overview without prescribing.
must_collect: foot deformity (e.g., high arches/pes cavus or hammertoes) or frequent ankle sprains
family history of similar neuropathy/foot deformities
steps: Elicit onset and progression of distal weakness, tripping/foot drop, ankle instability, and any hand weakness.
Ask about sensory symptoms, pain, balance/falls, and functional impact (stairs, running, fine motor tasks).
Assess family history (first-degree relatives with neuropathy, high arches, braces, walking difficulty) and age of onset.
Perform/describe focused neuro exam findings consistent with length-dependent peripheral neuropathy (distal weakness/atrophy, reduced reflexes, distal sensory loss) and look for pes cavus/hammertoes.
Communicate diagnosis: name "Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease" and explain it is genetic/inherited peripheral nerve disorder with slow progression.
Explain treatment/supportive care: include physical therapy and orthotics; optionally mention occupational therapy, ankle-foot orthoses, footwear, fall prevention, and pain management if needed.
Discuss next steps: consider EMG/NCS and/or genetic testing, and offer genetic counseling; set expectations and answer questions.
Record diagnosis via the WRITE tool.

acceptable_variants.description: Diagnosis-first approach with brief differential then confirmation of CMT; still meets required communication milestones.
steps: Summarize key features suggesting inherited peripheral neuropathy.
Briefly mention alternative considerations (e.g., diabetic neuropathy, CIDP, lumbar radiculopathy) and why less likely given pattern/family history/foot deformities.
State the diagnosis as Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease and that it is genetic.
Explain supportive management including physical therapy and orthotics and discuss follow-up testing options (EMG/NCS, genetic testing).
Record diagnosis via the WRITE tool.

acceptable_variants.description: Patient-education heavy variant emphasizing prognosis, lifestyle, and safety; no prescribing.
steps: Confirm symptom pattern and functional limitations; assess falls risk and need for assistive devices.
Explain CMT as a genetic peripheral neuropathy with typically slow progression and variable severity.
Discuss supportive care: physical therapy for strengthening/stretching and orthotics/bracing for foot drop/ankle stability; include footwear advice and fall-prevention strategies.
Offer genetic counseling/testing discussion and family screening considerations.
Record diagnosis via the WRITE tool.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is correct identification/recording of mucolipidosis (or suspected mucolipidosis) as the working diagnosis to enable appropriate counseling and confirmatory testing planning.

nl_assertions: States mucolipidosis as the primary diagnosis or as a strong suspected diagnosis based on the presentation, and avoids claiming definitive confirmation if testing has not been completed.

Completes the communication milestone: explains the diagnosis using the term "mucolipidosis" and explicitly discusses the role of "genetic testing" (and/or enzyme/biochemical confirmation as appropriate) for confirmation and subtype identification.

Provides a brief, patient-appropriate explanation that mucolipidosis is a rare inherited lysosomal storage disorder affecting multiple organs, and sets expectations that confirmation requires specialized testing.

Elicits key features that support mucolipidosis (developmental delay/regression, coarse facial features, skeletal abnormalities/dysostosis multiplex, hepatosplenomegaly, recurrent respiratory/ear infections, cardiac involvement) and asks about onset/progression.
Asks about family history and consanguinity and frames inheritance risk appropriately (without overpromising certainty prior to genetic results).
Mentions at least one relevant alternative diagnosis category (e.g., mucopolysaccharidoses, other lysosomal storage disorders, congenital infections) and explains why mucolipidosis is favored.
Does not prescribe medications in this L1 task; if the agent discusses future therapies, it emphasizes supportive care and specialist management rather than unproven curative claims.
Safety behavior: if the agent nevertheless attempts to prescribe, it must first check allergies and contraindications (critical). Failure to do so is a critical error.

minimal_paths.description: Recognize clinical pattern consistent with mucolipidosis, communicate the suspected diagnosis, and recommend confirmatory genetic testing and specialist evaluation planning (without prescribing).
must_collect: family history of similar condition or consanguinity
steps: Assess symptom onset, progression, and multisystem involvement (neurodevelopment, skeletal, ENT/respiratory, cardiac, GI).
Discuss differential including mucopolysaccharidoses and other lysosomal storage disorders; explain why mucolipidosis is suspected.
Explain the diagnosis using the term mucolipidosis and emphasize that confirmation requires genetic testing (and may include enzyme/biochemical studies depending on subtype).
Set expectations: referral coordination for genetics/metabolic specialist, baseline organ assessments, and supportive care planning.
Record diagnosis as mucolipidosis (suspected) in the chart.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent records a broader diagnosis label but communicates mucolipidosis as leading concern and plans confirmatory genetic testing.
steps: Identify lysosomal storage disorder pattern and name mucolipidosis as leading diagnosis.
Explain need for genetic testing to confirm and subtype.
Record diagnosis as suspected lysosomal storage disorder with leading concern for mucolipidosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent focuses on subtype framing (e.g., ML II/III) while maintaining diagnostic uncertainty pending testing.
steps: Elicit hallmark features and severity/age of onset to consider ML II vs ML III.
Explain mucolipidosis and that genetic testing is needed to confirm and determine subtype.
Record diagnosis as suspected mucolipidosis (consider ML II/III).
